text: {"matches":[{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":14,"toIndex":0,"rationale":"[14] mentions only challenges, while [0] includes both opportunities and challenges."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":5,"toIndex":62,"rationale":"Both list the same benefits: personalized learning, improved outcomes, and increased engagement."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":63,"toIndex":6,"rationale":"[63] lists a subset of challenges (over‑reliance, diminished thinking, academic fraud) omitting data‑privacy risks present in [6]."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":19,"toIndex":2,"rationale":"[19] specifies the sample as 85 second‑year engineering students, whereas [2] only notes 85 purposively selected participants."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":22,"toIndex":3,"rationale":"[22] provides a detailed breakdown of the 11‑item questionnaire, making it a more specific description than [3]."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":23,"toIndex":58,"rationale":"Both state that 95.6% of respondents use AI in academic activities."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":24,"toIndex":59,"rationale":"Both report that 88.2% of respondents use virtual assistants."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":27,"toIndex":30,"rationale":"Both indicate that 1.2% of students do not use AI tools at all."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":41,"toIndex":61,"rationale":"Both give the same proportion (48.2%) of students concerned about inaccurate AI‑generated content."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":52,"toIndex":54,"rationale":"Both describe excessive reliance on AI reducing critical thinking and independent problem‑solving."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":56,"toIndex":6,"rationale":"[56] describes a specific personal effect (reduced willingness to ask questions) whereas [6] lists broader over‑reliance challenges."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":50,"toIndex":46,"rationale":"[50] quantifies a 50% reduction in study hours, a more specific claim than the general time‑saving noted in [46]."},{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":41,"toIndex":51,"rationale":"[41] provides a concrete 48.2% figure of concern about incorrect answers, while [51] states the primary concern is reliability in general."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":11,"toIndex":16,"rationale":"Both claim AI improves engagement and outcomes, but refer to different technologies (adaptive learning systems vs intelligent tutoring systems)."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":11,"toIndex":17,"rationale":"[11] emphasizes feedback and performance; [17] focuses on content tailoring, so they address different aspects of AI‑driven platforms."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":46,"toIndex":47,"rationale":"Both discuss AI reducing time on tasks; [47] additionally highlights how this enables deeper critical thinking."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":53,"toIndex":41,"rationale":"[53] states information is often incorrect and needs verification, while [41] reports the proportion of students worried about such inaccuracies."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":8,"toIndex":6,"rationale":"[8] lists concerns about accuracy, cognitive disengagement, and ethics; [6] lists over‑reliance, critical‑thinking decline, privacy, and dishonesty, overlapping but distinct challenge sets."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":7,"toIndex":8,"rationale":"[7] proposes a structured framework and ethical guidelines; [8] enumerates concerns to be addressed, focusing on different implementation aspects."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":31,"toIndex":34,"rationale":"[31] reflects students’ belief that AI enhances the educational experience, while [34] measures belief that AI improves academic performance; the scopes differ (experience vs performance)."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":38,"toIndex":39,"rationale":"[38] reports a majority believing AI improves learning efficiency; [39] reports a minority disagreeing, both can coexist as differing student opinions."}]}
